md: In the previous lesson, the product rule fell out of *counting copies* of $a$. The quotient rule comes from the same idea ‚Äî except now we *cancel* matching copies. And one beautiful side effect of the quotient rule: it forces a value on $a^0$. We don't get to choose what $a^0$ means; consistency picks it for us.
### 1. Quotient rule ‚Äî when bases match, subtract exponents
What is $\dfrac{a^5}{a^2}$? Write out the copies and cancel:

blocks.tex: \dfrac{a^5}{a^2} = \dfrac{a \cdot a \cdot a \cdot a \cdot a}{a \cdot a} = a \cdot a \cdot a = a^3

md: Two copies cancelled on top, two on the bottom. Five copies minus two cancelled copies leaves three. In general:

blocks.title: Why $a \ne 0$?
blocks.body: Division by zero is undefined. As long as $a \ne 0$, the cancellation is legal. Every quotient-rule problem silently assumes this.

md: ### 2. Power of a quotient ‚Äî distribute the exponent
What is $\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)^3$? Three copies of the fraction:
Same logic as power-of-a-product ‚Äî the exponent distributes across the division:
### 3. The zero exponent ‚Äî forced by consistency

Here's something genuinely surprising. What should $a^0$ mean? "Multiply $a$ by itself zero times" is meaningless ‚Äî there are no copies. So we look at what value $a^0$ must take for the quotient rule to keep working.
Take $\dfrac{a^n}{a^n}$. We can evaluate it two ways:
Both expressions must be equal, so:

blocks.title: Not a separate rule ‚Äî a forced value
blocks.body: $a^0 = 1$ isn't a new convention bolted on. It's the *only* value $a^0$ can take if we want the quotient rule to keep working. Every extension of "powers" beyond positive integers comes from this same demand for consistency. (Curious about $0^0$? It's a story for another day ‚Äî see the blog post "Why a‚Å∞ = 1" for the full picture.)

md: ### Cheat sheet
| Rule | Formula | What you do |
| Quotient of powers | $\dfrac{a^m}{a^n} = a^{m-n}$ | Subtract exponents (top ‚àí bottom) |
| Power of a quotient | $\left(\dfrac{a}{b}\right)^n = \dfrac{a^n}{b^n}$ | Distribute across division |
| Zero exponent | $a^0 = 1$ | Any nonzero base to the 0 is 1 |
### Worked examples
**Example 2.** $\left(\dfrac{2}{3}\right)^4 = \dfrac{2^4}{3^4} = \dfrac{16}{81}$.
**Example 3 ‚Äî quotient of monomials.** $\dfrac{12 a^7 b^3}{4 a^2 b} = \dfrac{12}{4} \cdot a^{7-2} \cdot b^{3-1} = 3 a^5 b^2$.
**Example 4 ‚Äî applying $a^0 = 1$.** $\dfrac{x^5}{x^5} = x^{5-5} = x^0 = 1$. Or simply: anything (nonzero) over itself.

**Example 5 ‚Äî sneaky.** $(7 x^4 y^2)^0 = 1$. The *entire* expression (as long as it's nonzero) is being raised to the zero. So the whole thing collapses to $1$, regardless of how complicated it is inside.

blocks.title: Watch out ‚Äî order matters
blocks.body: $\dfrac{a^m}{a^n}$ gives $a^{m-n}$ (top exponent minus bottom). If $m < n$, the result has a *negative* exponent. We'll deal with those properly in the next lesson ‚Äî for now, just note that getting $a^{-3}$ instead of $a^3$ when you reverse the order is a sign that you swapped top and bottom.
